summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes LEAD, the first large-scale foundation model specifically for EEG-based AD detection, trained on a curated corpus of 2,238 subjects. It introduces a gated temporal-spatial Transformer that adapts to arbitrary recording lengths, channel configurations, and sampling rates, combined with a subject-regularized training strategy to enhance subject-level feature learning. The model was pre-trained using medical contrastive learning on 13 neurological disorder datasets and demonstrated superior performance across 5 downstream AD datasets compared to 16 baselines, including SOTA EEG foundation models.

headline_results: Outperformed 16 baselines including LaBraM and CBraMod
Achieved best average ranking across 20 evaluations

key_points: New EEG foundation model: LEAD is designed for subject-level Alzheimer's disease detection, utilizing a gated temporal-spatial Transformer to handle heterogeneous EEG data.
Novel training paradigm: Employs medical contrastive pre-training on 13 datasets and a subject-regularized strategy featuring subject-level cross-entropy loss and index group shuffling.
Strong empirical results: Achieves the best average ranking across 20 evaluations on 5 downstream AD datasets, surpassing existing SOTA EEG foundation models.
limitations: Limited performance on dementia-stage classification (AD vs MCI vs HC) compared to binary detection.
Difficulty distinguishing MCI subjects due to overlapping distributions of dementia stages.
Lack of longitudinal EEG data to track disease progression.

method.architecture: Gated temporal-spatial Transformer with 3D channel and sampling rate embeddings
method.finetuning: Fine-tuned using a combination of sample-level and subject-level cross-entropy losses
method.objective: Sample- and subject-level medical contrastive learning

summary.one_liner: A large-scale EEG foundation model for Alzheimer's disease detection utilizing a gated temporal-spatial Transformer and subject-regularized training.
summary.title: LEAD: An EEG Foundation Model for Alzheimer's Disease Detection
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces the world's largest EEG-AD corpus and a gated temporal-spatial Transformer with subject-regularized training for robust subject-level Alzheimer's detection.

papers.title: LEAD: An EEG Foundation Model for Alzheimer's Disease Detection

reasons: Explicitly proposes LEAD as a 'large-scale foundation model for EEG-based AD detection'
Uses a pre-training strategy on a large-scale corpus of 13 different EEG datasets
Demonstrates broad transferability by fine-tuning and testing on 5 downstream AD datasets
Directly compares performance against other state-of-the-art EEG foundation models

summary.detailed_summary: FEMBA proposes a self-supervised framework using bidirectional state-space models (Mamba) to overcome the quadratic time and memory complexity of Transformer-based EEG models. The model is pre-trained on over 21,000 hours of unlabeled EEG data from 5,000 subjects using a masked reconstruction objective. Evaluation on TUAB, TUAR, and TUSL benchmarks demonstrates that FEMBA achieves competitive or state-of-the-art performance while significantly reducing computational overhead, particularly in its Tiny variant.
key_points: New EEG foundation model: FEMBA utilizes a bidirectional Mamba architecture to provide linear-time complexity for processing extended EEG recordings.
Pre-trained on a massive scale of 21,000+ hours of unlabeled EEG from 5,000+ subjects using a 60% masked reconstruction task.
Demonstrates extreme efficiency, with the Tiny variant (7.8M params) beating previous SOTA on artifact detection (TUAR) while reducing computation by 27x.
limitations: Performance gaps in AUPR on TUSL likely due to class imbalance.
Domain shifts between neonatal and adult EEG require further adaptation.
Need for further exploration of SSM-specific interpretability mechanisms.
Lack of multi-modal physiological signal integration.

method.architecture: Bidirectional Mamba encoder with a 2D convolutional tokenizer
method.finetuning: Linear or Mamba-enhanced classifiers for downstream tasks
method.objective: Self-supervised masked reconstruction (60% masking) using Smooth L1 loss
method.pretraining: Pre-trained on 21,000+ hours of unlabeled EEG data

summary.one_liner: A bidirectional Mamba-based foundation model for EEG that achieves linear scaling and high efficiency for clinical and wearable applications.
summary.title: FEMBA: Efficient and Scalable EEG Analysis with a Bidirectional Mamba Foundation Model
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a bidirectional Mamba architecture for EEG foundation modeling that scales linearly with sequence length, enabling high-performance analysis on resource-constrained wearable devices.

papers.title: FEMBA: Efficient and Scalable EEG Analysis with a Bidirectional Mamba Foundation Model

reasons: Explicitly identifies as a 'Bidirectional Mamba Foundation Model' for EEG.
Utilizes a self-supervised framework trained on a large-scale dataset of 21,000 hours of unlabeled EEG.
Demonstrates broad transferability by fine-tuning on multiple downstream tasks for general-purpose analytics.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes a cross-biosignal transfer learning framework that fine-tunes the CEReBrO EEG foundation model for blood pressure estimation from ECG and PPG waveforms. Unlike prior work that trains from scratch or uses modality-specific pre-training, this approach leverages temporal representations learned from EEG to improve accuracy and convergence. Evaluations on MIMIC-III and VitalDB show the model achieves near state-of-the-art results, with dynamic INT8 quantization reducing the model size by up to 3.9x while maintaining performance.

headline_results: MIMIC-III: DBP MAE 1.57 mmHg, SBP MAE 2.72 mmHg
VitalDB: DBP MAE 1.92 mmHg, SBP MAE 3.14 mmHg

key_points: Application of an EEG foundation model: Uses the CEReBrO transformer, pre-trained on EEG, to estimate systolic and diastolic blood pressure from ECG and PPG signals.
Cross-modality transfer: Unfrozen fine-tuning of the EEG backbone significantly outperforms training from scratch and frozen-backbone approaches in terms of MAE and convergence.
Hardware optimization: Employs dynamic INT8 quantization to reduce model size by ~3.5-3.9x, enabling real-time inference on resource-constrained wearables.
limitations: Data heterogeneity across patient demographics and clinical conditions may affect generalizability.
Real-time on-device latency and battery constraints on ultra-low-power microcontrollers are not yet fully evaluated.
Susceptibility to real-world confounders like motion artifacts and signal dropouts.
Lack of patient-specific calibration for daily-life continuous monitoring.

method.architecture: CEReBrO transformer-encoder with an added MLP regression head
method.finetuning: Unfrozen backbone fine-tuning on ECG/PPG signals

summary.one_liner: Adapts a pre-trained EEG foundation model for cuffless blood pressure estimation using ECG and PPG signals with post-training quantization for wearable deployment.
summary.title: Finetuning and Quantization of EEG-Based Foundational BioSignal Models on ECG and PPG Data for Blood Pressure Estimation
summary.unique_contribution: Demonstrates the first successful transfer of a large-scale EEG foundation model to cuffless blood pressure estimation using ECG and PPG signals without additional large-scale pre-training.

papers.title: Finetuning and Quantization of EEG-Based Foundational BioSignal Models on ECG and PPG Data for Blood Pressure Estimation

reasons: Explicitly utilizes 'EEG-based foundation models' as the starting point for transfer learning.
Investigates the transferability of representations learned from abundant EEG data to other biosignals (ECG/PPG).
Focuses on the adaptation and fine-tuning of an EEG-FM, fitting the ecosystem criteria for EEG-FM evaluation and transfer.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes the Large Cognition Model (LCM), a transformer-based foundation model designed to learn universal EEG representations. It introduces a learnable channel mapping to align diverse electrode configurations and a self-supervised framework combining spatio-temporal contrastive alignment with masked patch reconstruction. Evaluations on BCIC-2A and BCIC-2B motor imagery datasets demonstrate that LCM outperforms existing universal models such as EEGPT and LaBraM.

headline_results: Outperforms EEGPT by 3.2% in balanced accuracy on BCIC-2A and 3.11% on BCIC-2B.

key_points: New EEG foundation model: LCM employs a transformer architecture with learnable channel mapping to unify diverse electrode configurations into a common latent space.
Method: Uses a self-supervised learning strategy that integrates spatio-temporal contrastive alignment and masked feature reconstruction.
Results: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on BCIC-2A and BCIC-2B benchmarks, surpassing models like EEGPT and LaBraM in balanced accuracy and Cohen's Kappa.
limitations: Requires larger and more diverse pretraining datasets to further boost generalization.
Spatial and temporal feature extraction could be refined for better interpretability.
Does not currently integrate multimodal biosignals.

method.architecture: Transformer with a convolutional block and a learnable channel mapping matrix for montage alignment
method.finetuning: Fine-tuning on BCIC-2A and BCIC-2B datasets for 100 epochs
method.objective: Combined spatio-temporal contrastive alignment (MSE) and masked patch reconstruction loss
method.pretraining: Self-supervised pretraining on PhysioMI, TSU, and SEED datasets

summary.one_liner: A transformer-based EEG foundation model utilizing learnable channel mapping and a dual contrastive-reconstruction objective for cross-dataset generalization.
summary.title: Large Cognition Model: Towards Pretrained EEG Foundation Model
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a transformer-based EEG encoder with learnable channel mapping and a combined contrastive-reconstruction objective to enable robust generalization across different EEG montages and tasks.

papers.title: Large Cognition Model: Towards Pretrained EEG Foundation Model

reasons: Explicitly proposes a 'transformer-based foundation model' designed to generalize across diverse EEG datasets and tasks.
Utilizes large-scale self-supervised learning to capture 'universal EEG representations'.
Focuses on broad transferability and efficient fine-tuning for multiple downstream EEG applications.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes SynthSleepNet, a framework that leverages EEG, EOG, EMG, and ECG signals using a combination of generative (masked prediction) and discriminative (contrastive) SSL objectives. It differs from prior work by integrating these two SSL paradigms and employing a Mamba-based temporal context module to efficiently capture inter-epoch dependencies. The model's effectiveness is demonstrated on the SHHS1 dataset across sleep-stage classification, apnea, and hypopnea detection tasks, showing superior performance even in semi-supervised settings.
key_points: New EEG foundation model: SynthSleepNet uses a hybrid SSL approach combining masked prediction and contrastive learning across multiple PSG modalities.
Integrates a Mamba-based temporal context module to capture long-range dependencies across sleep epochs more efficiently than LSTMs or Transformers.
Achieves state-of-the-art results on sleep-stage classification (89.89% acc) and apnea detection (99.75% acc) using the SHHS1 dataset.
limitations: Relatively lower performance in hypopnea detection compared to other tasks.
Dependence on specific modalities like ECG for subtle event detection.
Lack of respiratory-specific modalities such as airflow or thoracic effort belts.
Need for greater dataset diversity to improve generalizability.
Requirement for lightweight optimization for real-time clinical application.

method.architecture: Modality-specific backbones (NeuroNet-based) with LoRA, a ViT-based multimodal encoder, and ViT decoders.
method.finetuning: Incorporates a Mamba-based Temporal Context Module (TCM) to model inter-epoch dependencies.
method.objective: Joint loss of MSE for masked prediction and NT-Xent for contrastive learning.
method.pretraining: Hybrid SSL combining masked prediction and contrastive learning.

summary.one_liner: A multimodal hybrid self-supervised learning framework for PSG analysis combining masked prediction and contrastive learning with a Mamba-based temporal context module.
summary.title: Toward Foundational Model for Sleep Analysis Using a Multimodal Hybrid Self-Supervised Learning Framework
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a hybrid SSL framework that integrates masked prediction and contrastive learning to learn expressive multimodal representations of PSG data, enhanced by a Mamba-based temporal context module.

papers.title: Toward Foundational Model for Sleep Analysis Using a Multimodal Hybrid Self-Supervised Learning Framework

reasons: Title explicitly frames the work as 'Toward Foundational Model for Sleep Analysis'.
Uses a hybrid self-supervised learning framework (masked prediction and contrastive learning) to learn expressive representations.
Demonstrates transferability across multiple downstream tasks including sleep-stage classification, apnea detection, and hypopnea detection.
EEG is a core modality within the multimodal PSG data used for the pretrained model.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes TFM-Tokenizer, a framework that converts single-channel EEG signals into discrete tokens by learning a vocabulary of time-frequency motifs. It utilizes a dual-path architecture featuring a Localized Spectral Window Encoder and a Temporal Encoder, trained via a joint time-frequency masking objective. Evaluation across four clinical benchmarks and an ear-EEG sleep staging task demonstrates significant gains in accuracy and cross-device scalability, while also boosting the performance of existing foundation models like BIOT and LaBraM.

headline_results: Up to 11% gain in Cohen's Kappa on TUEV
14% improvement on ear-EEG sleep staging over baselines

key_points: New EEG tokenization method: TFM-Tokenizer learns a discrete vocabulary of time-frequency motifs from single-channel signals, making it model-agnostic and device-flexible.
Dual-path architecture: Combines a localized spectral window encoder with a temporal encoder using a joint masking objective to capture robust neural motifs.
Strong empirical results: Achieves up to 11% improvement in Cohen's Kappa on TUEV and demonstrates 14% better performance on out-of-domain ear-EEG sleep staging.
limitations: Fixed windowing can cause large neural patterns to be split across windows.
Temporal shifts may lead to separate token assignments for the same event.
Window boundaries may cause misinterpretation of continuous neural events.

method.architecture: Dual-path encoder (Localized Spectral Window Encoder and Temporal Encoder) followed by a Temporal Transformer and a VQ codebook quantizer.
method.finetuning: Masked token prediction on a downstream transformer followed by task-specific fine-tuning.
method.objective: Joint frequency-temporal masking prediction (masked reconstruction and vocabulary loss).

summary.one_liner: A model-agnostic tokenization framework that learns discrete time-frequency motifs from single-channel EEG to enhance foundation model representations.
summary.title: Tokenizing Single-Channel EEG with Time-Frequency Motif Learning
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a single-channel, device-agnostic tokenization method that explicitly learns a discrete vocabulary of time-frequency motifs to replace raw EEG inputs in foundation models.

papers.title: Tokenizing Single-Channel EEG with Time-Frequency Motif Learning

reasons: Explicitly addresses the challenge of EEG tokenization specifically for foundation models.
Designed as a plug-and-play component to enhance existing EEG-FMs such as BIOT and LaBraM.
Evaluated across multiple benchmarks and pretraining settings to improve generalizable EEG representations.

summary.title: An Efficient Self-Supervised Framework for Long-Sequence EEG Modeling

papers.title: An Efficient Self-Supervised Framework for Long-Sequence EEG Modeling

reasons: EEG is the primary modality and the central focus of the work.
Proposes a self-supervised pre-training framework (EEGM2) for EEG representation learning.
Explicitly claims strong generalization across multiple tasks and transferability across domains, aligning with foundation model goals.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper investigates whether general-purpose time series foundation models, specifically MOMENT, UniTS, and OTiS, can serve as effective feature extractors for EEG analysis across age prediction, seizure detection, and event classification. The authors compare these models against 16 specialized EEG models using zero-shot, linear probing, and fine-tuning strategies. The results demonstrate that general-purpose models are competitive and can localize demographic and disease-related biomarkers through frequency band analysis, reducing the reliance on large task-specific datasets.

headline_results: General-purpose models are competitive with specialized EEG models
Fine-tuning is required for age prediction

key_points: Benchmark study: Evaluates general-purpose time series foundation models (MOMENT, UniTS, OTiS) on EEG tasks, finding them competitive with specialized models.
Domain adaptation: Zero-shot features suffice for visually interpretable tasks like seizure detection, while fine-tuning is essential for complex tasks like age prediction.
Biomarker localization: Demonstrates that these models can identify frequency-specific biomarkers, such as age-related information in high frequencies and ictal activity in low frequencies.
limitations: Fine-tuning remains essential for complex clinical tasks that require high specialization.
Evaluation is limited to three general-purpose time series models.
Task-specific training on very limited data still risks overfitting.
Reliance on public datasets which may not capture the full diversity of clinical EEG scenarios.

method.architecture: General-purpose time series foundation models (MOMENT, UniTS, OTiS)

summary.one_liner: Evaluates the effectiveness of general-purpose time series foundation models as feature extractors for various EEG clinical tasks.
summary.title: Are foundation models useful feature extractors for electroencephalography analysis?
summary.unique_contribution: Systematically benchmarks general-purpose time series foundation models against specialized EEG models to assess their utility as feature extractors and their ability to localize clinical biomarkers.

papers.title: Are foundation models useful feature extractors for electroencephalography analysis?

reasons: Explicitly evaluates the utility of foundation models as feature extractors for EEG analysis.
Fits the ecosystem criteria as a benchmark/evaluation study of foundation models applied to EEG.
Focuses on the transferability of pretrained representations to reduce reliance on task-specific EEG datasets.
